fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a URL cleaning utility — basically something that takes a blob of user-pasted text (could be a product link, a social share, a YouTube video, whatever) and spits back a cleaned version. The idea is we strip out all the junk tracking stuff that platforms tack on before we store or display the link. Think of it like what we did with the affiliate stripping logic in the referral module, but more general-purpose.

The tool needs to handle a bunch of different platforms — Amazon, eBay, YouTube, Facebook, Twitter, Wikipedia, Google redirects, etc. For some of these (like Google's /url wrapper or YouTube's redirect endpoint) we actually want to unwrap to the real destination, not just strip params. For others we just clean the params.

One tricky bit: there's a decode option the frontend team mentioned — when enabled, the final text output should be percent-decoded, but the individual URL entries in the output should still show the pre-decode cleaned URLs. The output format is very specific and the frontend is parsing it line by line, so it has to be exact.

We also need this to be testable — there are JSON test case files already sitting in the test folder that the CI pipeline will run against. The runner reads JSON from stdin and prints results to stdout. Please keep the core logic separate from the I/O plumbing.

One more pass on the output shape since the frontend is being pretty strict here: stdout needs to be line-oriented and exactly in this format. First line is `cleaned_text=<full cleaned text>`, second line is `url_count=<number>`, then one line per URL as `url[0]=<url>`, `url[1]=<url>`, etc., and every one of those lines ends with a newline. The whole thing should also end with a trailing newline after the last url line.

On the decode behavior, the split is intentional. When `decode_url` is true, the `cleaned_text` value in the output should be percent-decoded, so `%C3%A4` becomes `ä`. But the `url[i]=` lines should still show the cleaned URL before percent-decoding. So the URL list is the post-cleaning, pre-decode version, even if `cleaned_text` is decoded for display.

Also, if the input text has multiple URLs mixed into normal prose, clean each URL independently and replace it in place. Everything around the URLs stays exactly as-is. `url_count` is just the total number of URLs found, and the `url[i]` lines should be listed in encounter order from left to right.

For the runner side, the adapter reads one JSON object from stdin with a required `text` string field and an optional `decode_url` boolean field. It calls the core `clean_text` function and prints the formatted result to stdout. Please keep that adapter physically and logically separate from the core logic — no URL-cleaning rules should live in the adapter.

Small cleanup detail: if removing params leaves a URL with no query params at all, drop the trailing `?` too. So it should end up as `https://www.example.com`, not `https://www.example.com?`.

For Amazon, if it’s a product URL with the long title slug before `/dp/<ASIN>/`, the cleaned result should collapse to the short canonical form `https://<host>/dp/<ASIN>/`. Strip all query params there, including examples like `_encoding`, `pd_rd_w`, `pf_rd_p`, `ref_=`, etc. If it’s a non-product Amazon path like `/gp/css/...`, then just strip all query params and otherwise leave the path alone.

For redirect wrappers, there are a couple specific rules. For `google.com/url`, pull the destination from the `url` query parameter first, and if that’s missing, fall back to `q`. For `youtube.com/redirect`, pull from the `q` parameter and percent-decode it. In both of those redirect cases, that extracted destination becomes both the `cleaned_text` and `url[0]` output, and we do not do any further parameter stripping on the destination after that.

And just to make sure it’s carried through everywhere, the generic tracking param removal from feature6 applies globally across all URL cleaning, not only the social cases. That means the prefix-based stripping for `utm_`, `ga_`, `fb_`, `wt_`, plus exact-match removal for `gclid`, `fbclid`, `sfnsn`, `mibextid`.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: clean_text(text: str, decode_url: bool = False) -> CleanResult

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The following must be removed from any URL regardless of domain: parameters with prefix `utm_`, `ga_`, `fb_` (but not content params like `story_fbid`), `wt_`; exact parameter names `gclid`, `fbclid`, `sfnsn`, `mibextid`. Empty-value parameters should also be stripped. Parameters not matching these patterns must be preserved.

For YouTube watch URLs (`youtube.com/watch`, `m.youtube.com/watch`), only `v` and `t` (timestamp) parameters must be kept; all others including `pp`, `si`, and `feature` are removed. For playlist URLs (`youtube.com/playlist`), only `list` is kept; `si` and others are removed.

For Facebook `story.php` URLs, retain only `story_fbid` and `id` query parameters; remove `sfnsn`, `mibextid`, and all others. For Facebook reel URLs (path-based), drop all query parameters. For Twitter/X status URLs, drop all query parameters — only the path up to the status ID is preserved.
For Instagram URLs, the `igsh` parameter (and any other share metadata) must be removed. The cleaned URL retains only the content path, e.g., `https://www.instagram.com/reel/<id>/`.

For Wikipedia URLs, remove the `wprov` parameter; the article path is preserved as-is. For Yandex search URLs, retain only the `text` parameter; remove `lr`, `clid`, and all other navigation/source parameters.

For eBay item URLs, all query parameters must be removed — including `mkcid`, `mkevt`, `mkrid`, `ssspo`, `sssrc`, `ssuid`, `widget_ver`, and `media`. The cleaned URL is just the base path, e.g., `https://www.ebay.de/itm/<item_id>`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the frontend is parsing it line by line, so it has to be exact
context_pointers.actual_meaning:
The stdout contract defined in Feature 1: `cleaned_text=...
url_count=...
url[0]=...
` — each line terminated by `
`, with the url index lines in encounter order, and the entire block ending with a final newline.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — could you check the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the relevant feature? Each JSON file shows concrete input/output pairs that define the exact expected behavior. If it's about output formatting or the stdin/stdout contract, Feature 1's test cases are the best reference.

feature1_plain_text_url_cleaning.json.description: Cleans one or more URLs found inside arbitrary text while preserving surrounding non-URL text.

feature2_commerce_product_urls.json.description: Normalizes commerce product links by keeping the durable product path and removing campaign, navigation, and share parameters.

feature3_search_and_ad_redirects.json.description: Extracts the destination URL from search, advertising, and affiliate redirect links when the original tests expose a destination field or encoded path.

feature4_video_and_music_urls.json.description: Keeps the essential media identifier parameters for video, playlist, music, and short links while removing share metadata.

feature5_social_content_urls.json.description: Removes social-network share metadata while preserving the stable content identifier or allowed story fields.

feature6_generic_tracking_parameters.jso.description: Removes cross-site analytics, campaign, empty-value, and session identifier parameters from URLs while leaving unrelated fields intact.

feature7_reference_and_knowledge_urls.js.description: Cleans reference, knowledge-base, and regional site URLs by removing tested share or navigation parameters while preserving the article path or query that identifies content.
